notes.note: Tophet was a place in the valley of Hinnom near Jerusalem where children were burned in sacrifice (2 Kings 23:10; Jeremiah 7:31), so accursed by the prophets that its name became a common word for hell. Among the gallows-shaped sign, the black pots, and the landlady named Coffin, Tophet is the darkest of the death-omens Ishmael half-jokingly reads into the inn.

notes.anchor: now extinct as the ancient Medes
notes.note: The Medes were a people of ancient Iran who recur through the Old Testament, in Daniel, Esther, and Isaiah, often paired with the Persians as a once-great empire long since vanished. Melville reaches for them as a scriptural byword for a nation reduced to nothing, pressing the point that the Pequot people have been wiped out just as utterly.

notes.anchor: the spines of the three old kings of Cologne
notes.note: The three kings of Cologne are the Magi, the wise men of Matthew 2, whose reputed relics have been enshrined in Cologne Cathedral since the twelfth century and drew pilgrims for centuries. The simile casts the Pequod's worn mast-stumps as ancient and venerable relics, half-sacred and half-absurd.

notes.anchor: Captain Bildad, if I had followed thy advice
notes.note: Bildad is named for one of Job's three friends (Job 2:11) who visit him in his suffering and deliver long, reproving speeches insisting his misery must be punishment for sin. The pious, penny-pinching part-owner earns the name, mixing scripture with hard bargaining while comforting no one.

notes.anchor: Lay not up for yourselves treasures upon earth, where moth
notes.note: Bildad quotes the Sermon on the Mount, Matthew 6:19-21, on laying up treasure in heaven rather than on earth, where moth and rust corrupt. Melville turns it into a pun: lay is also the whaling word for a sailor's fractional share of the voyage's profit, so Bildad's piety and his haggling collapse into a single word.

notes.anchor: Ahab of old, thou knowest, was a crowned king
notes.note: Ahab, king of Israel (1 Kings 16-22), is the Bible's model of a ruler who did evil in the sight of the Lord above all before him. Peleg recalls that dogs licked Ahab's blood (1 Kings 22:38), the fulfillment of Elijah's curse, a detail that quietly hangs a prophesied doom over the captain who bears his name.

notes.anchor: remain not for aye a Belial bondsman
notes.note: Belial in the Hebrew Bible is a word for worthlessness or wickedness (Deuteronomy 13:13), and in 2 Corinthians 6:15 Paul sets Christ against Belial, so that the name came to stand for the devil. Bildad is urging Queequeg to stop being a bondsman, a slave, of Satan.

notes.anchor: Spurn the idol Bell, and the hideous dragon
notes.note: Bildad alludes to Bel and the Dragon, an addition to the Book of Daniel found in Catholic and Orthodox Bibles, in which Daniel exposes the idol Bel as a fraud and destroys a worshipped dragon. The Quaker uses the old story as shorthand for idol-worship, aimed at Queequeg's little black god.

Elijah was the prophet who confronted King Ahab of Israel and pronounced the curse that dogs would lick his blood (1 Kings 21). The chapter is titled The Prophet, and the ragged stranger who gives that name as his own greets Ishmael with the same kind of ominous half-warning that Elijah carried to a doomed king.

notes.anchor: grinding the face of the poor
notes.note: The phrase is from Isaiah 3:15, where God rebukes the rulers of Israel for beating his people and grinding the faces of the poor. Ishmael aims this thunderous prophetic accusation at the small comic problem of Queequeg perched on a sleeping rigger, so the borrowed gravity lands as a joke.

notes.anchor: miserably drives along the leeward land
notes.note: A lee shore is a coast on the side toward which the wind blows, so a ship near it is being pushed straight onto the rocks; caught there in a gale, a vessel often cannot beat clear and is wrecked. The phrase was proverbial among sailors for the most dangerous of all positions, which is why Bulkington keeps to the open sea.

Apotheosis comes from the Greek for making into a god and means the raising of a mortal to divine rank, as Roman emperors were declared gods after death. Melville recasts Bulkington's death at sea not as a drowning but as such an ascent, ending this short chapter on a deliberately exalted note.
